narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the model zeroed in on a few key elements from your profile that shaped this outcome. Most influential was your Year 1 grade decile of 4, which falls well below the average of 6 for students who passed the bar; this stood out as the strongest signal pushing the prediction toward failure, as lower early performance often correlates with struggles later on. Next, your LSAT score of 35, compared to the 37.27 average among bar passers, further nudged the model toward expecting a fail, highlighting a gap in that critical benchmark. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile of 5 is close to the 5.91 average for successful students, which helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing and showed your improvement over time. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 actually exceeds the 3.24 average for those who passed, and your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of bar passers, but these weren't the top drivers here. Being full-time also matches the norm for successful students. Overall, your profile compares unfavorably to typical admitted students mainly due to that early decile and LSAT, despite strengths in later academics and background. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, as even a modest improvement could shift future predictions dramatically. Strengthening early-grade equivalents through additional coursework or targeting schools with more flexible thresholds might also help. We encourage reapplying after addressing these areas—many students turn predictions around with targeted effort.
